questions.text: A pendulum bob takes 8.6s to go from X to Y and back to X ten times. What is the period of the pendulum?
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: The period of a pendulum is the time it takes for one complete oscillation, from one extreme point to the other and back. In this case, the pendulum goes from point X to Y and then back to X, completing one full oscillation.Given that the pendulum takes 8.6 seconds to complete ten oscillations, you can find the period (\(T\)) by dividing the total time by the number of oscillations:\[T = \frac{\text{Total time}}{\text{Number of oscillations}} = \frac{8.6 \, \text{s}}{10} = 0.86 \, \text{s}\]The period of the pendulum is 0.86 seconds, which corresponds to option 0.86 s.

questions.text: A particle oscillates up and down in simple harmonic motion. Its height y as a function of time t is shown in t the diagram. At what t time t does the particle achieve its maximum positive acceleration?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Acceleration occurs when the mass is at negative displacements since the force will be acting in the opposite direction of the displacement to restore equilibrium. Based on F=kAx the most force, and therefore the most acceleration occurs where the most displacement is.
At 2 s, the particle is moving through the equilibrium position where the displacement and, consequently, the acceleration is zero.
At 3 s, the particle is at its maximum positive displacement, where the acceleration is maximum but in the negative direction.
At 4 s, similar to 2 s, the particle is again passing through the equilibrium position with zero displacement and acceleration.

questions.explanation: In simple harmonic motion, the acceleration of a particle is directly proportional to its displacement from the equilibrium position and acts in the opposite direction. The equation a = -ω²x describes this relationship, where a is the acceleration, ω is the angular frequency, and x is the displacement. Maximum positive acceleration occurs when the particle is at its maximum negative displacement. Therefore, at 1 s, the particle is at its maximum negative displacement, resulting in the maximum positive acceleration. Other options represent positions where the particle is either at equilibrium (2 s and 4 s) or at maximum positive displacement (3 s), which corresponds to maximum negative acceleration.

questions.text: Under the action of the restoring force:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The speed of the body always increases
options.explanation: The speed of the body always increases. This is incorrect because the restoring force is always directed towards the equilibrium position. This means that the restoring force will always act to slow down the body as it approaches its equilibrium position.

options.text: The body moves at constant speed
options.explanation: The body moves at constant speed. This is also incorrect because the restoring force will always act to change the speed of the body. The speed of the body will only be constant if the restoring force is balanced by another force, such as friction.

The body always slows down. This is incorrect because the restoring force will not always act to slow down the body. The restoring force will only act to slow down the body as it approaches its equilibrium position.

The body accelerates under the action of the restoring force. The restoring force is a force that acts to bring a body to its equilibrium position. The restoring force is always directed towards the equilibrium position, and it is proportional to the displacement of the body from its equilibrium position. When a body is displaced from its equilibrium position, the restoring force will act to bring the body back to its equilibrium position. This will cause the body to accelerate towards its equilibrium position. The acceleration of the body will be greatest when the body is farthest from its equilibrium position, and it will decrease as the body approaches its equilibrium position.

questions.explanation: Correct option is D.Under the action of the restoring force, the body accelerates because the force acts to bring the body back to its equilibrium position. This force is proportional to the displacement from equilibrium, as described by Hooke's Law (F = -kx), leading to acceleration (a = F/m = -ω^2x).Option A is incorrect because the speed does not always increase; it depends on the direction of motion relative to the equilibrium.Option B is incorrect because the speed is not constant; it varies as the body moves in response to the restoring force.Option C is incorrect because the body does not always slow down; it speeds up when moving toward the equilibrium position from a point of maximum displacement.

questions.text: If the maximum velocity of SHM is ao , the period of oscillation is _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The correct answer is A) 2πxo/ao. In simple harmonic motion (SHM), the maximum velocity (vmax) is related to the amplitude (xo) and angular frequency (ω) as vmax = ωxo. The period of oscillation (T) is defined as the time taken for one complete cycle of the motion. It is related to the angular frequency as T = 2π/ω. To find the relationship between the maximum velocity (vmax) and the period (T), we can substitute vmax = ωxo into the equation T = 2π/ω: T = 2π/ω = 2π/(vmax/xo) = 2πxo/vmax. Given that the maximum velocity (vmax) is ao, the equation becomes: T = 2πxo/ao.
Incorrect. This option incorrectly places ao in the numerator and xo in the denominator, which does not match the derived formula for the period T = 2πxo/ao.
Incorrect. This option multiplies ao and xo, which is not consistent with the formula for the period. The correct relationship involves dividing xo by ao.
Incorrect. This option suggests dividing by both ao and xo, which does not reflect the correct formula for the period T = 2πxo/ao.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A) 2πxo/ao. In simple harmonic motion (SHM), the maximum velocity (vmax) is related to the amplitude (xo) and angular frequency (ω) as vmax = ωxo. The period of oscillation (T) is described by the equation T = 2π/ω. By substituting the expression for ω from vmax = ωxo, we get T = 2π/(vmax/xo) = 2πxo/vmax. Since the maximum velocity (vmax) is given as ao, we substitute it into the equation to get T = 2πxo/ao. Other options do not correctly reflect this relationship: B) inverts the placement of xo and ao, C) incorrectly multiplies ao and xo, and D) suggests dividing by both ao and xo, none of which mathematically represent the period of SHM.

questions.text: A simple harmonic oscillator has a time period of 10 seconds. Which equation rotates its acceleration ‘a’ and displacement ‘x’?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option incorrectly assumes a direct proportionality without considering the angular frequency factor.
This option incorrectly uses an arbitrary constant instead of calculating the angular frequency from the time period.

The motion of a simple harmonic oscillator can be described by the following equation:a = -ω^2 * xwhere 'a' is the acceleration, 'x' is the displacement, and ω is the angular frequency of the oscillator.The time period of the oscillator is given by:T = 2π/ωwhere 'T' is the time period.In this case, the time period is given as 10 seconds. Therefore, we can find the angular frequency as:ω = 2π/ T = 2π /10 =π/5Substituting this value of ω in the equation of motion, we get:a = - (π /5)^2 * xSimplifying, we get:a = - (2π /10)^2 * xTherefore, the correct option is C) alpha = - (2π / 10) ^ 2 * x, where 'alpha' represents acceleration.
This option not only uses an incorrect constant but also squares it unnecessarily, leading to an incorrect expression.

questions.explanation: In simple harmonic motion (SHM), the acceleration 'a' of a particle is related to its displacement 'x' by the formula: a = -ω2 * xwhere ω is the angular frequency. The angular frequency ω is given by:ω = 2π/Twhere 'T' is the time period of the oscillator. In this problem, T = 10 seconds, so:ω = 2π/10 = π/5Substituting ω into the acceleration formula gives:a = -(π/5)2 * x = -(2π/10)2 * xTherefore, the correct answer is Option C. The other options either ignore the angular frequency or misuse constants, leading to incorrect expressions for acceleration.

questions.text: What should be the ratio of kinetic energy to total energy for simple harmonic oscillator?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: KE/E = 1 - (x^2/xo^2)Where x is the displacement from the equilibrium position and xo is the amplitude of the oscillation.This means that the ratio of kinetic energy to total energy is given by 1 minus the square of the displacement divided by the square of the amplitude.Therefore, the correct answer is:1 - (x^2/xo^2)
1: This option represents a ratio of 1, indicating that the kinetic energy is equal to the total energy for a simple harmonic oscillator. However, this is not the correct ratio.

(xo^2 - x^2): This option represents the difference between the square of the amplitude (xo^2) and the square of the displacement (x^2). However, this is not the correct ratio of kinetic energy to total energy.
1/2 * (x^2): This option represents half of the square of the displacement (x^2). This expression is not the correct ratio of kinetic energy to total energy.

questions.explanation: 𝐾𝐸 = 1 2 𝑚ω 2 (𝑥 0 2 − 𝑥 2 ) 𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝐸𝑛𝑒𝑟𝑔𝑦 = 1 2 𝑚ω 2 𝑥 0 2 Dividing the two terms gives us the ratio and the solution to the equationKE/E = 1 - (x^2/xo^2)Where x is the displacement from the equilibrium position and xo is the amplitude of the oscillation.This means that the ratio of kinetic energy to total energy is given by 1 minus the square of displacement divided by the square of the amplitude.Therefore, the correct answer is:1 - (x^2/xo^2)

questions.text: The velocity and acceleration of a particle performing S.H.M. have a steady phase relationship. The acceleration showed a phase lead over the the velocity of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: While a phase difference of π radians could imply that one function is the negative of the other, it does not apply to the phase difference between velocity and acceleration in S.H.M.

Displacement=x x= a sin wt And velocity=v v= dx/dt =aw cos wt..(i) Acceleration=a a= d2x/dt2 = dv/dt = -aw2sin wt a= aw2 cos ( wt + 𝜋/2) …(ii) [ ➨cos ( θ+ 𝜋/2)= -sinθ ] By comparing eq i and ii we can conclude that acceleration leads velocity by a phase of + 𝜋/2 radian.
A phase difference of -π/2 indicates that velocity would lead acceleration, which is not the case in S.H.M. where acceleration leads velocity.
Similar to the explanation for π, a phase difference of -π suggests a complete phase inversion, which is incorrect for the relationship between velocity and acceleration in S.H.M.

questions.explanation: In simple harmonic motion (S.H.M.), the displacement x is given by x = a sin(ωt). The velocity v, being the first derivative of displacement with respect to time, is v = aω cos(ωt). The acceleration a, the derivative of velocity, is given by a = -aω^2 sin(ωt), which can also be expressed as a = aω^2 cos(ωt + π/2). This shows that acceleration leads velocity by a phase of π/2 radians. The other options are incorrect because they represent different or opposite phase relationships that do not apply to the phase relationship between velocity and acceleration in S.H.M.

K.E=½ mw2 (x02-x2) = ½ mw2 (x02- x02sin2 wt) =½ mw2 x02(1-sin2 wt) =½ mw2 x02cos2 wt =½ mw2 X02(1+cos2 wt/2 ) =¼ mw2 x02 +¼ mw2 x02cos2wt =2f Hence the frequency of K.E. is 2ω or 2f In SHM, a particle has zero or minimum velocity at extreme points and maximum velocity at mean points. During one complete oscillation, the kinetic energy will become maximum twice. Therefore the frequency of kinetic energy will be 2f.

questions.explanation: K.E=½ mw2(x02-x2) = ½ mw2 (x02- x02sin2 wt) =½ mw2 x02(1-sin2 wt) =½ mw2 x02cos2 wt =½ mw2 x02(1+cos2 wt/2 ) =¼ mw2 x02 +¼ mw2 x02cos2wt =2f Hence the frequency of K.E. is 2ω or 2f In SHM, a particle has zero or minimum velocity at extreme points and maximum velocity at mean points. With the help of a diagram, we can see that in one time period of the pendulum (starting from the left extreme point) K.E. reaches zero to a maximum of two times. During one complete oscillation, the kinetic energy will become maximum twice. Therefore the frequency of kinetic energy will be 2f.

questions.text: A 0.5 kg mass is attached to a spring, causing the spring to stretch by 0.2 meters. If the spring constant is 100 N/m, what is the force exerted by the spring?

questions.text: A body of mass 4 kg attached to a spring is displaced through 0.04 m from its equilibrium position and then released. If the spring constant is 400 N/m, find the time period of the vibration?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This calculation assumes an incorrect formula or incorrect substitution of values. The correct formula is T = 2π * √(m/k).

The time period of vibration can be calculated using the formula: T = 2π * √(m/k) where T is the time period, m is the mass of the body, and kis the spring constant. In this case, the mass of the body is 4 kg and the springconstant is 400 N/m. T = 2π * √(4 kg / 400 N/m) T = 2π * √(0.01 s^2/kg) T = 2π * 0.1 s T ≈ 0.628 s Therefore, the time period of vibration for the bodyattached to the spring is approximately 0.628 seconds.

questions.explanation: This is the solution to this question: The time period of vibration can be calculated using the formula: T = 2π * √(m/k) where T is the time period, m is the mass of the body and kis the spring constant. In this case, the mass of the body is 4 kg, and the springconstant is 400 N/m. T = 2π * √(4 kg / 400 N/m) T = 2π * √(0.01 s^2/kg) T = 2π * 0.1 s T ≈ 0.628 s Therefore, the time period of vibration for the bodyattached to the spring is approximately 0.628 seconds.

questions.text: Two springs fixed at one end are stretched by 5 cm and 10 cm, respectively, when masses 0.5 kg and 1 kg are suspended at their lower ends. When displaced slightly from their mean positions and released, they will oscillate with time periods in the ratio
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The time period of a spring-mass system is given by T = 2π√(m/k), where T is the time period, m is the mass, and k is the spring constant. Since the spring constant is the same for both springs (as the same force produces the same extension), the ratio of the time periods will only depend on the ratio of the masses: T1/T2 = √(m1/m2). Substituting the given masses, we get T1/T2 = √(0.5 kg / 1 kg) = √(1/2) = 1/√2.
This is incorrect. The ratio of the time periods is not directly proportional to the ratio of the masses. Instead, it depends on the square root of the mass ratio.
This is incorrect. The ratio of the time periods is not inversely proportional to the ratio of the masses.
This is incorrect. The square root of the mass ratio is 1/√2, not √2.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. The time period of a spring-mass system is given by T = 2π√(m/k), where T is the time period, m is the mass, and k is the spring constant. Since the spring constant is the same for both springs (as the same force produces the same extension), the ratio of the time periods will only depend on the ratio of the masses: T1/T2 = √(m1/m2). Substituting the given masses, we get T1/T2 = √(0.5 kg / 1 kg) = √(1/2) = 1/√2.

questions.text: To double the total energy for a mass-spring system oscillating in SHM, by what factor must the amplitude increase?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The energy depends on the square of the amplitude, so quadrupling the amplitude would increase the energy by a factor of 16.
This is incorrect. Doubling the amplitude would increase the energy by a factor of 4, not 2.

The total energy of a mass-spring system in SHM is given by:E = (1/2)kA^2where k is the spring constant and A is the amplitude of oscillation.To double the total energy, we need:2E = (1/2)k(A')^2where A' is the new amplitude.Solving for A', we get:A' = A * sqrt(2)Therefore, the amplitude must increase by a factor of sqrt(2) to double the total energy.So the answer is (c) underroot 2 = 1.414.
This is incorrect. Increasing the amplitude by the fourth root of 2 would not double the energy.

questions.explanation: The total energy of a mass-spring system in simple harmonic motion (SHM) is given by the formula: E = (1/2)kA^2, where k is the spring constant and A is the amplitude of oscillation. To double the total energy, you set up the equation: 2E = (1/2)k(A')^2 and solve for the new amplitude A'. Solving, we find A' = A * √2. Therefore, the amplitude must increase by a factor of √2 to double the total energy.Option A (4) suggests that quadrupling the amplitude is required, which would increase the energy by a factor of 16, not 2. Option B (2) suggests doubling the amplitude, which would increase the energy by a factor of 4. Option D (∜2) suggests an incorrect factor for doubling the energy. Therefore, the correct answer is Option C, √2.

questions.text: The speed V of the waves in the string depends upon the tension F of the string and m, the mass per unit length of the string. it is given by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: V^2 = F/m: This equation suggests that the square of the wave speed (V^2) is equal to the tension (F) divided by the mass per unit length (m). Hence, this equation is correct.

It states that the wave speed (V) is equal to the tension (F) divided by the mass per unit length (m). This equation reflects the direct proportionality between wave speed and tension, while inversely proportional to the mass per unit length.However, the correct relation should either have V^2 or square root of F/m.

V * m = F: This equation suggests that the product of wave speed (V) and the mass per unit length (m) is equal to the tension (F). However, this equation is not correct. The correct equation does not involve multiplication between wave speed and mass per unit length.

V = F * m: This equation suggests that the wave speed (V) is equal to the product of tension (F) and mass per unit length (m). However, this equation is not correct. The correct equation involves dividing the tension by the mass per unit length, not multiplying them.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: V2 = F/m. This equation accurately represents the physical relationship where the square of the wave speed is determined by the tension in the string divided by the mass per unit length. This formula is derived from fundamental principles of wave mechanics. Option B is incorrect because it does not square the speed as required by the law. Option C incorrectly suggests multiplication instead of division, and Option D suggests an incorrect relationship by multiplying tension and mass per unit length, which does not apply in this context.

In a simple harmonic motion of a mass-spring system, the potential and kinetic energy are continuously converting into one another, but the total energy remains constant, represented by a straight line in diagram D.

questions.explanation: In a mass-spring system undergoing simple harmonic motion (SHM), potential energy and kinetic energy are constantly converting into one another. However, the total mechanical energy of the system remains constant throughout the motion, as there is no energy loss to friction or other external forces. Diagram D correctly represents this principle with a straight line, indicating consistent total energy. The other options (A, B, and C) suggest varying energy levels, which do not accurately depict the conservation of total energy in SHM.

So let us assume that instead of observer, the Pendulum is moving at 0.95 c and the observer is at rest .- Using the formula- The time dilation formula is given by,- T =T0 /√1−(v2/c2)where,- T is the time observed- T0 is the time observed at rest- v is the velocity of the object- c is the velocity of light in a vacuum (3 × 108 m/s2)T= 3/√1−[(0.95c)2/c2]- T= 3/√1−(0.90c2/c2)- T= 3/√1-0.90- T= 3/√0.10- Take 0.10 to 0.9 to solve easily- T= 3/√0.9- T= 3/0.3- T= 10 seconds 9.6 seconds

questions.text: When frequency of applied periodic Force is equal to the natural frequency of harmonic oscillator then the _ of oscillator _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The amplitude increases rather than decreasing.

When the frequency of the applied periodic force is equal to the natural frequency of a harmonic oscillator, the amplitude of the oscillator reaches its maximum. In this case, the oscillator is said to be in resonance. Resonance occurs when the driving frequency matches the natural frequency of the oscillator, resulting in a significant increase in the amplitude of the oscillations. During resonance, the applied force synchronizes with the natural frequency of the oscillator, leading to a maximum transfer of energy. The oscillations of the system become larger and can build up over time, reaching their highest possible amplitude. It's important to note that resonance can be both desirable and undesirable, depending on the application. In some cases, resonance is beneficial, such as in musical instruments where it helps produce rich, sustained tones. However, in other situations, resonance can be problematic, leading to excessive vibrations or potential damage to the system. Engineers often need to consider and mitigate the effects of resonance in various designs to prevent undesirable consequences. This question refers to the phenomenon of resonance. During resonance, the amplitude keeps on increasing after every subsequent oscillation, provided the system is undamped.
The frequency remains the same.
The frequency remains the same.

questions.explanation: This question refers to the phenomenon of resonance. During resonance, the amplitude keeps on increasing after every subsequent oscillation, provided the system is undamped.

questions.text: For a simple harmonic motion the acceleration of the body is _ to displacement and always directed towards _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: For a simple harmonic motion, the acceleration of the body is directly proportional to displacement and always directed towards the equilibrium position.In simple harmonic motion, the acceleration (a) of the body is given by the equation:a = -ω^2xWhere:ω (omega) represents the angular frequency of the motion,x represents the displacement of the body from the equilibrium position.The negative sign indicates that the acceleration is in the opposite direction of the displacement. This means that when the body is displaced from the equilibrium position, the acceleration will be directed towards the equilibrium position.

questions.explanation: For a simple harmonic motion, the acceleration of the body is directly proportional to the displacement and always directed towards the equilibrium position.In simple harmonic motion, the acceleration of the body is given by the equation:a = -ω²xwhere:a is the acceleration,x is the displacement from the equilibrium position, andω is the angular frequency of the motion.The negative sign indicates that the acceleration is directed towards the equilibrium position. This means that when the body is displaced from its equilibrium position, it experiences an acceleration that acts in the opposite direction, pulling it back towards the equilibrium. As the body moves towards the equilibrium position, the acceleration decreases until it reaches zero at the equilibrium point. Then, as the body moves away from the equilibrium in the opposite direction, the acceleration reverses its direction and continues to oscillate back and forth.The two conditions that have to be met for an oscillatory motion to be simple harmonic are:1- The acceleration is directly proportional to displacement.2- The displacement is always directed opposite to the direction of acceleration, i.e towards the mean position.

questions.text: A force F = 0.12 N is applied to a spring and the spring elongates by 3 cm. The specific constant of the spring is?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a spring is stretched/compressed by the application of a force, the relationship between the magnitude of the force applied and the elongation of the spring is given by Hooke's law:F=kxWhere F is the magnitude of the spring appliedk is the spring constantx is the elongation of the spring relative to its equilibrium positionFor the spring in this problem, we have:F = 0.12 N (force applied)x = 3 cm = 0.03 m (elongation of the spring)Therefore, we can solve the formula for k to find the spring constant:k=F/x k= 0.12/0.03k= "4N/m"

When a spring is stretched/compressed by the application of a force, the relationship between the magnitude of the force applied and the elongation of the spring is given by Hooke's law:F=kxWhere F is the magnitude of the spring appliedk is the spring constantx is the elongation of the spring relative to its equilibrium positionFor the spring in this problem, we have:F = 0.12 N (force applied)x = 3 cm = 0.03 m (elongation of the spring)Therefore, we can solve the formula for k to find the spring constant:k=F/x k= 0.12/0.03k= "4N/m"

When a spring is stretched/compressed by the application of a force, the relationship between the magnitude of the force applied and the elongation of the spring is given by Hooke's law:F=kxWhere F is the magnitude of the spring appliedk is the spring constantx is the elongation of the spring relative to its equilibrium positionFor the spring in this problem, we have:F = 0.12 N (force applied)x = 3 cm = 0.03 m (elongation of the spring)Therefore, we can solve the formula for k to find the spring constant:k=F/x k= 0.12/0.03k= "4N/m"

When a spring is stretched/compressed by the application of a force, the relationship between the magnitude of the force applied and the elongation of the spring is given by Hooke's law:F=kxWhere F is the magnitude of the spring appliedk is the spring constantx is the elongation of the spring relative to its equilibrium positionFor the spring in this problem, we have:F = 0.12 N (force applied)x = 3 cm = 0.03 m (elongation of the spring)Therefore, we can solve the formula for k to find the spring constant:k=F/x k= 0.12/0.03k= "4N/m"

questions.explanation: When a spring is stretched/compressed by the application of a force, the relationship between the magnitude of the force applied and the elongation of the spring is given by Hooke's law: F=kx Where F is the magnitude of the spring applied k is the spring constant x is the elongation of the spring relative to its equilibrium position For the spring in this problem, we have: F = 0.12 N (force applied) x = 3 cm = 0.03 m (elongation of the spring) Therefore, we can solve the formula for k to find the spring constant: k=F/x k= 0.12/0.03 k= "4N/m"

questions.text: An airplane is flying in a straight line at a constant altitude. If a wind gust strikes and raises the nose of the airplane, the nose will bob up and down until the plane eventually returns to its original position. These oscillations are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Undamped oscillations would continue indefinitely without any decrease in amplitude. In the context of the airplane's motion, undamped oscillations would mean that the nose of the airplane continues to bob up and down without ever settling back to its original position. This is not the behavior described in the scenario, so option a) is incorrect.

Underdamped oscillations refer to oscillations that gradually decrease in amplitude over time and return to their equilibrium position. This matches the behavior described in the scenario, where the nose of the airplane bobs up and down before eventually returning to its original position. Therefore, option b) is correct.

Critically damped oscillations describe a scenario where oscillations return to equilibrium as quickly as possible without any overshooting. This behavior is not described in the scenario, so option c) is incorrect.

Overdamped oscillations occur when the system takes an extended period to return to equilibrium without oscillating around it. This behavior is not described in the scenario, so option d) is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In the context of the scenario described, option b) "Underdamped" is the correct answer, as it accurately characterizes the behavior of the airplane's nose bobbing up and down before returning to its original position.

questions.text: Equation of SHM, with amplitude ‘a’ is given by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: X = a(sin2 ω t + cos2 ω t)
options.explanation: This equation doesn't resemble the standard form of SHM. It appears to be a combination of sine and cosine functions, but it doesn't represent simple harmonic motion.

options.text: X = a(sin ω t cos2 ω t)
options.explanation: Similar to option (a), this equation doesn't match the standard form of SHM. It combines a sine and cosine function with a cosine squared term, which is not typical of SHM.

the equation of SHM with amplitude 'a' is given by:X = a sin(wt)where:X is the displacement of the object from its equilibrium positiona is the amplitude (maximum displacement)w is the angular frequency (2π times the frequency)Therefore, the correct answer is option C: X = a sin(wt)
This equation deviates significantly from the standard form of SHM. It incorporates a sine function of a sine function, which is not characteristic of simple harmonic motion.

questions.explanation: X = a sin ω t is the standard equation of SHM, in which a corresponds to amplitude.

questions.text: To double the total energy for a mass-spring system oscillating in SHM, by what factor must the amplitude increase?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The energy depends on the square of the amplitude, so quadrupling the amplitude would increase the energy by a factor of 16.
This is incorrect. Doubling the amplitude would increase the energy by a factor of 4, not 2.

The total energy of a mass-spring system in SHM is given by:E = (1/2)kA^2where k is the spring constant and A is the amplitude of oscillation.To double the total energy, we need:2E = (1/2)k(A')^2where A' is the new amplitude.Solving for A', we get:A' = A * sqrt(2)Therefore, the amplitude must increase by a factor of sqrt(2) to double the total energy.So the answer is (c) underroot 2 = 1.414.
This is incorrect. Increasing the amplitude by the fourth root of 2 would not double the energy.

questions.explanation: The total energy of a mass-spring system in simple harmonic motion (SHM) is given by the formula: E = (1/2)kA^2, where k is the spring constant and A is the amplitude of oscillation. To double the total energy, you set up the equation: 2E = (1/2)k(A')^2 and solve for the new amplitude A'. Solving, we find A' = A * √2. Therefore, the amplitude must increase by a factor of √2 to double the total energy.Option A (4) suggests that quadrupling the amplitude is required, which would increase the energy by a factor of 16, not 2. Option B (2) suggests doubling the amplitude, which would increase the energy by a factor of 4. Option D (∜2) suggests an incorrect factor for doubling the energy. Therefore, the correct answer is Option C, √2.

questions.text: When the frequency of the applied force becomes equal to one of natural frequencies of body then the body oscillates with maximum displacement this phenomenon is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Heating refers to the process of increasing the temperature of an object or substance. It occurs when energy is transferred to the object, typically resulting in an increase in kinetic energy of its particles, leading to higher temperature. In the context of oscillations and resonance, heating may occur due to friction or other dissipative processes that convert mechanical energy into thermal energy.

Resonance is the phenomenon that occurs when the frequency of an external force applied to a system matches the natural frequency of the system. This results in the system oscillating with maximum amplitude, as energy is efficiently transferred to the system. Resonance can occur in various systems, including mechanical oscillators, electrical circuits, and acoustic systems. In mechanical systems, resonance can lead to structural vibrations or even structural failure if not properly damped.

Reverberation refers to the persistence of sound in an enclosed space due to repeated reflections off surfaces. It is commonly experienced in rooms with hard and reflective surfaces, such as concert halls or empty rooms. Reverberation can affect the clarity and quality of sound, and it is often controlled or mitigated in acoustic design to optimize the acoustic environment.

Damping is the process by which energy is dissipated or removed from a vibrating system, causing its amplitude to decrease over time. Damping can occur due to various factors such as friction, air resistance, or energy transfer to other modes of vibration. It is essential for controlling oscillations and preventing excessive vibration in mechanical systems. In the context of resonance, damping is necessary to prevent the system from oscillating uncontrollably at resonance frequencies.

questions.explanation: Resonance describes the phenomenon of increased amplitude that occurs when the frequency of a periodically applied force is equal or close to a natural frequency of the system on which it acts.
hints: frequency of the applied force becomes equal to one of natural frequencies

questions.text: For a stiff spring, the value of spring constant is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to Hooke's law, the force F required to extend or compress a spring by some distance x is directly proportional to that distance, with the constant of proportionality being the spring constant k. Mathematically, we can write:F = kxFrom this equation, we can see that if the force required to extend or compress the spring is high for a given distance, then the value of k must also be high. Therefore, for a stiff spring, the spring constant is higher.
This statement is incorrect. A lower value of spring constant would indicate a less stiff spring, not a stiff spring.
This statement is also incorrect. A stiff spring is characterized by a higher spring constant, not an intermediate value.
This statement is incorrect as well. A spring with a zero spring constant would not exhibit any resistance to deformation and would not behave like a spring at all.

questions.explanation: According to Hooke's law, the force F required to extend or compress a spring by some distance x is directly proportional to that distance, with the constant of proportionality being the spring constant k. Mathematically, we can write:F = kxFrom this equation, we can see that if the force required to extend or compress the spring is high for a given distance, then the value of k must also be high. Therefore, for a stiff spring, the spring constant is higher.

questions.text: For a simple harmonic motion the acceleration of the body is _ to displacement and always directed towards _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: For a simple harmonic motion, the acceleration of the body is directly proportional to displacement and always directed towards the equilibrium position.In simple harmonic motion, the acceleration (a) of the body is given by the equation:a = -ω^2xWhere:ω (omega) represents the angular frequency of the motion,x represents the displacement of the body from the equilibrium position.The negative sign indicates that the acceleration is in the opposite direction of the displacement. This means that when the body is displaced from the equilibrium position, the acceleration will be directed towards the equilibrium position.

questions.explanation: For a simple harmonic motion, the acceleration of the body is directly proportional to the displacement and always directed towards the equilibrium position.In simple harmonic motion, the acceleration of the body is given by the equation:a = -ω²xwhere:a is the acceleration,x is the displacement from the equilibrium position, andω is the angular frequency of the motion.The negative sign indicates that the acceleration is directed towards the equilibrium position. This means that when the body is displaced from its equilibrium position, it experiences an acceleration that acts in the opposite direction, pulling it back towards the equilibrium. As the body moves towards the equilibrium position, the acceleration decreases until it reaches zero at the equilibrium point. Then, as the body moves away from the equilibrium in the opposite direction, the acceleration reverses its direction and continues to oscillate back and forth.The two conditions that have to be met for an oscillatory motion to be simple harmonic are:1- The acceleration is directly proportional to displacement.2- The displacement is always directed opposite to the direction of acceleration, i.e towards the mean position.

questions.text: Damping is the process whereby _ is _ from the oscillating system.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Damping is the process whereby energy is dissipated or removed from the oscillating system.In a system undergoing oscillatory motion, such as simple harmonic motion, there may be external forces or internal factors that cause the system to lose energy over time. This energy loss is known as damping. Damping can occur due to various mechanisms, including friction, air resistance, or the internal resistance of the system itself.When damping is present, the amplitude of the oscillations gradually decreases over time. This is because the energy of the system is continuously being converted into other forms, such as heat or sound, and is dissipated from the system. Eventually, the oscillations come to a stop when all the energy is dissipated.Damping can have different effects on the behavior of the oscillating system. It can modify the frequency of the oscillations, causing it to decrease over time. It can also affect the shape of the oscillations, leading to changes in the waveform.In some cases, damping is intentionally introduced to control or dampen the oscillations, such as in shock absorbers or damping mechanisms in mechanical systems. However, in other situations, damping may be an undesirable effect that needs to be minimized to maintain the desired oscillatory behavior.

questions.explanation: In undamped motion, energy remains constant since there is no work done against the damping force. In damped oscillatory motion, there is work done against the damping force (friction) , hence, the overall energy is dissipated.

questions.text: In stretched string, the speed of wave is independent of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The speed of a wave on a stretched string is directly proportional to the square root of the tension. Therefore, tension affects the wave speed.
The length of the string does directly influence the speed of the wave.
The total mass of the string influences the wave speed through mass per unit length, which is a factor in calculating wave speed.

options.text: The point from where the string is plucked
options.explanation: The location where the string is plucked does not affect the speed of the wave; it affects the mode of vibration.

questions.explanation: The speed of a wave on a stretched string depends on the tension in the string and the mass per unit length. It is calculated using the formula v = √(T/μ), where T is the tension and μ is the mass per unit length. Thus, the wave speed is independent of the length of the string and the point from where the string is plucked. The point of plucking affects the initial conditions of the wave but not its speed. Therefore, both options B and D are correct.

questions.text: If 30 waves per second pass through a medium at a speed of 30 ms-1 then the wavelength is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect according to calculations.
Given data: v=30ms-1, f=30HzFind out: λ=?Solution:The number of waves passing through a point per second in known as frequency. So, f = 30Hzv = fλ λ = v/f = 30/30 = 1m
This option is incorrect according to calculations.
This option is incorrect according to calculations.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.Given data: v=30ms-1, f=30HzFind out: λ=?Solution:The number of waves passing through a point per second in known as frequency. So, f = 30Hzv = fλ λ = v/f = 30/30 = 1m

questions.text: Which of the following becomes maximum at the mean position?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Potential Energy (P.E.): The potential energy of the oscillating object is not maximum at the mean position. At the mean position, the potential energy is actually at its minimum because the object is at its equilibrium point.

Acceleration (a): The acceleration is zero at the mean position because the net force acting on the object is zero at this point, not maximum. Acceleration is maximum at the extreme positions where the object changes direction.
Displacement (x): The displacement from the mean position is zero at the mean position, not maximum. Displacement is maximum at the extreme positions of the oscillation.

Kinetic Energy (K.E.): The kinetic energy is maximum at the mean position because the velocity is maximum here. As the object passes through the mean position, its velocity is the highest, making its kinetic energy also the highest.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is K.E, as kinetic energy is maximum at the mean position. In simple harmonic motion, the mean position is where the object has its highest velocity and hence its highest kinetic energy. The formula for kinetic energy is K.E. = 1/2 * m * v². At the mean position, velocity is maximum, which makes kinetic energy maximum. Other energy forms like potential energy are at their minimum at this point. The potential energy is maximum at the extreme positions where the object is momentarily at rest. Similarly, acceleration is maximum at the extreme positions and zero at the mean position, while displacement is zero at the mean position.

questions.text: A mass attached to an elastic spring is executing SHM with a frequency of 5 Hz, its angular velocity is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer because the angular velocity ω is calculated using ω = 2πf, where f = 5 Hz. Therefore, ω = (2)(π)(5) = 10π rads-1.
This option is incorrect. It is a miscalculation of the formula ω = 2πf. The frequency is 5 Hz, not 0.1 Hz.
This option is incorrect because it doubles the correct value of ω. The frequency is not 10 Hz, but 5 Hz.
This option is incorrect because it assumes a frequency of 1 Hz instead of 5 Hz. ω is calculated as 2πf.

questions.explanation: The angular velocity ω in simple harmonic motion is given by the formula ω = 2πf, where f is the frequency. Given the frequency f = 5 Hz, we substitute this into the formula to get ω = 2π(5) = 10π rads-1. Therefore, the correct answer is 10π rads-1.Option A is correct because it follows the correct calculation. Option B is incorrect because it miscalculates the formula, assuming a much lower frequency. Option C incorrectly doubles the frequency, leading to a value of 20π, which is incorrect. Option D calculates ω for a frequency of 1 Hz rather than the given 5 Hz, thus yielding an incorrect result of 2π.

questions.text: The time period of a body attached to a spring depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time period of a body attached to a spring does not depend on its amplitude. The amplitude refers to the maximum displacement of the body from its equilibrium position in Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M). The time period of S.H.M depends on other factors, but not on the amplitude.
The time period of a body attached to a spring depends on its mass. In general, for a mass-spring system, the larger the mass of the body, the longer the time period of its oscillation.

The time period of a body attached to a spring does not depend on its length. The length of a spring itself does not affect the time period of oscillation. The time period depends on other factors such as mass and spring constant.

The time period of a body attached to a spring does not depend on its displacement either. The time period of oscillation remains constant for a given mass-spring system, regardless of the initial displacement from the equilibrium position.

questions.explanation: In summary, the correct option is: b) Mass Time period of body attached to a spring is directly proportional to square root of mass.

questions.text: In an isolated system the total energy of vibrating mass and spring is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the total energy of a vibrating mass and spring in an isolated system is variable, it means that the energy can change over time. However, in an isolated system, the total energy is conserved, meaning it remains constant. So, this option is not correct.

If the total energy of a vibrating mass and spring in an isolated system is low, it implies that the energy is not sufficient. However, the total energy in an isolated system is determined by the initial conditions and remains constant throughout the vibrations. So, this option is not correct.

If the total energy of a vibrating mass and spring in an isolated system is high, it suggests that there is a large amount of energy involved. However, in an isolated system, the total energy remains constant and does not change. So, this option is not correct.

The correct option! In an isolated system, the total energy of a vibrating mass and spring remains constant. This means that the energy is neither increasing nor decreasing but remains the same throughout the vibrations.

questions.explanation: (a) Variable: If the total energy of a vibrating mass and spring in an isolated system is variable, it means that the energy can change over time. However, in an isolated system, the total energy is conserved, meaning it remains constant. So, this option is not correct. (b) Low: If the total energy of a vibrating mass and spring in an isolated system is low, it implies that the energy is not sufficient. However, the total energy in an isolated system is determined by the initial conditions and remains constant throughout the vibrations. So, this option is not correct. (c) High: If the total energy of a vibrating mass and spring in an isolated system is high, it suggests that there is a large amount of energy involved. However, in an isolated system, the total energy remains constant and does not change. So, this option is not correct. (d) Constant: The correct option! In an isolated system, the total energy of a vibrating mass and spring remains constant. This means that the energy is neither increasing nor decreasing but remains the same throughout the vibrations. So, the correct option is (d) constant, as the total energy in an isolated system of a vibrating mass and spring remains constant.

questions.text: If the period of oscillation of mass (M) suspended from a spring is 2s,then the period of mass 4M will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To achieve a period of 1 second, the mass would need to decrease, not increase as in this case.
This value represents the original period with mass M, not 4M.
This is an intermediate value and does not result from the mass being quadrupled.
Correct. The period is doubled because the mass is quadrupled, resulting in 2 times the original period.

questions.explanation: The period of a mass-spring system is given by the formula: T = 2π √(m/k), where T is the period, m is the mass, and k is the spring constant.For a mass M, the period T = 2 seconds: 2 = 2π √(M/k).For a mass 4M, the new period T' is: T' = 2π √((4M)/k).Dividing the equation for T' by the equation for T:T' / T = √((4M)/k) / √(M/k) = √4 = 2Thus, T' = 2 * T = 4 seconds. Hence, the period for 4M is 4 seconds.The other options are incorrect because:1s: This would imply a decrease in mass, not an increase.2s: This is the period for the original mass M, not 4M.3s: This value does not reflect the relationship between mass and period in this context.

questions.text: The displacement of the particle executing SHM is given by x = 0.01 sin (100πt). The time period is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The angular frequency is not correctly used to calculate the time period.
This option is incorrect. The calculation does not correctly apply the formula T = 2π/ω.
This is the correct answer. Using the formula T = 2π/ω, where ω = 100π, the calculation gives T = 1/50 = 0.02 sec.
This option is incorrect. Ensure the correct application of the formula T = 2π/ω.

questions.explanation: The displacement of the particle in simple harmonic motion is given by x = 0.01 sin (100πt). The standard form for SHM is x = x0 sin (ωt), where ω is the angular frequency.By comparing the given equation with the standard form, we identify ω = 100π. The time period T is related to ω by the formula T = 2π/ω.Substituting ω = 100π into the formula gives:T = 2π / (100π) = 1/50 = 0.02 sec.Therefore, the correct answer is 0.02 sec. The other options result from incorrect calculations or misunderstandings of the formula T = 2π/ω.

questions.text: A simple harmonic oscillator consists of an particle of mass "m" and an ideal spring with spring constant "k". li the spring is cut in half and used with the same particle, the period will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Doubling the period suggests that the system oscillates slower. Cutting the spring in half actually increases the spring constant, which would decrease the period.
When the spring is cut in half, the spring constant doubles, which results in the period being reduced by a factor of √2.
Increasing the period by a factor of √2 suggests a decrease in spring constant, which is incorrect when the spring is halved.
Keeping the period the same suggests that halving the spring has no effect, which is incorrect because the spring constant changes.

questions.explanation: In a simple harmonic oscillator, the period T is given by the formula T = 2π√(m/k). When the spring is cut in half, its spring constant k doubles (since k' = 2k for a spring cut in half). Therefore, the new period T' becomes T' = 2π√(m/(2k)) = T/√2. Thus, the correct answer is T/√2. The other options are incorrect as they either imply no change (Option D), a decrease in spring constant (Option A), or an increase in the period (Option C), which do not align with the change in spring constant when the spring is cut in half.

questions.text: The angular frequency "ω" and frequency "f" of SHM are related:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests that frequency is 2π times the angular frequency, which is not correct.
This is the correct relationship between angular frequency ω and frequency f in simple harmonic motion.
This option incorrectly implies that frequency is the inverse of 2π times the angular frequency, which is not correct.
This option incorrectly suggests that frequency is the inverse of angular frequency, which is not correct.

questions.explanation: The relationship between angular frequency (ω) and frequency (f) in simple harmonic motion is given by ω = 2πf. This is derived from the fact that one complete oscillation corresponds to an angle of 2π radians, and frequency is the reciprocal of the period (T), i.e., f = 1/T. Therefore, ω = 2π/T = 2πf.Option A suggests a relationship where frequency is 2π times the angular frequency, which is incorrect. Option C implies that frequency is the inverse of 2π times the angular frequency, which is also incorrect. Option D incorrectly suggests that frequency is simply the inverse of angular frequency.

questions.text: The time period of a simple pendulum is 2 seconds. If its length is increased by 4 times, then its period becomes:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please refer to the main explanation text.
Please refer to the main explanation text.
Please refer to the main explanation text.
Please refer to the main explanation text.

questions.explanation: The formula for the period of a simple pendulum is: T = 2π √(L/g) Where: T is the period of the pendulum, L is the length of the pendulum, and g is the acceleration due to gravity. In this case, we have a pendulum with a period of 2 seconds. So, we can substitute these values into the formula: 2 = 2π √(L/g) Now, let's consider the length of the pendulum increased by 4 times. Let's call the new length L'. So, we have: T' = 2π √(L'/g) To compare the periods, we can divide the two equations: T' / T = (2π √(L'/g)) / (2π √(L/g)) Simplifying this equation, we get: T' / T = √(L'/g) / √(L/g) T' / T = √(L') / √L Since the length is increased by 4 times, we can write L' = 4L: T' / T = √(4L) / √L T' / T = 2√L / √L T' / T = 2 Therefore, the period of the pendulum becomes 2 times the original period. Since the original period is 2 seconds, the new period will be 2 * 2 = 4 seconds.

questions.text: The total energy of a block, executing simple harmonic motion is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The total energy in SHM is independent of x.Because E=1/2(kA2), it stays constant no matter where the block is in its motion. Only kinetic and potential energy change with x, but their sum (total energy) does not.

If energy were proportional to x, then when the block is at the mean position (x=0), total energy would vanish.But in reality, at mean position, kinetic energy is maximum, so total energy is still constant and not zero.

The total energy in SHM is independent of x.Because E=1/2(kA2), it stays constant no matter where the block is in its motion. Only kinetic and potential energy change with x, but their sum (total energy) does not.

This option suggests that the total energy of the block is proportional to the square root of the displacement 'x.' However, this is incorrect. The total energy of a block in simple harmonic motion is not directly proportional to the square root of the displacement.

questions.explanation: The total energy in SHM is independent of x.Because E=1/2(kA2), it stays constant no matter where the block is in its motion. Only kinetic and potential energy change with x, but their sum (total energy) does not.

questions.text: The force applied on a unit area to produce any change in the volume or length of a body is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect. Strain is a measure of the deformation or change in shape or size of a body relative to its original shape or size, not the force applied.
Correct. Stress is the force applied per unit area to a material. It is responsible for causing deformation or changes in the volume or length of a body.

Incorrect. Elasticity is the property of a material to return to its original shape or size after the removal of the deforming force. It is not the force applied but rather the material's response to stress.

Incorrect. Equilibrium refers to a state of balance where the net force and net torque acting on an object are zero. It does not specifically refer to the force applied to produce changes in volume or length.

questions.explanation: In physics, stress is defined as the force applied per unit area to a material. It is the external force that acts on a body to deform it or cause changes in its volume or length. Stress is an important concept in the study of mechanics and materials science. Therefore, option b) stress is the correct choice.

questions.text: A mass hangs on a string fixed at point P. It starts from position 1 and swings to the furthest position on the opposite side, position 2. It then oscillates several times with decreasing amplitude before ending at position 3.Where does the ball have the most kinetic energy?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At position 1, the ball is at the maximum amplitude. Hence, it is stationary at that particular instant. It has no speed and no kinetic energy at that point.
At position 3, the ball is in the equilibrium position. Thus it is moving at the maximum speed for that specific oscillation. So it has maximum kinetic energy at that point.

The ball would be moving at its highest speed at position 3 for THAT PARTICULAR OSCILLATION ONLY. Progressivley as the ball oscillates, the speed decreases. Hence it would not have the maximum kinetic energy at position 3 the last time.

questions.explanation: At positions 1, 2 and finally (last time) coming to rest at 3, the speed of the ball is zero. The ball will undergo a few oscillations before coming to rest and so the speed of the ball when it is the first time at 3 will be maximum hence the kinetic energy will be maximum.

questions.text: A second pendulum has time period of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A second pendulum is defined to have a time period of 2 seconds, not 1 second.
Correct! A second pendulum is one that has a time period of exactly 2 seconds.
A time period of 4 seconds is too long for what defines a second pendulum.
This time period is incorrect for a second pendulum, which is specifically defined to have a 2-second period.

questions.explanation: A second pendulum is a pendulum with a time period of 2 seconds—this means it takes 2 seconds to complete one full oscillation (back and forth). This is a standard definition in physics. Option A is incorrect because 1 second is half the period of a second pendulum. Options C and D are incorrect because they exceed the time period that defines a second pendulum.

questions.text: To double the period of a pendulum, the length:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Must be increased by a factor 2
options.explanation: Increasing the length by a factor of 2 multiplies the period by sqrt(2), not 2.

options.text: Must be decreased by a factor 2
options.explanation: Decreasing the length by a factor of 2 will decrease the period, not double it.

options.text: Must be increased by a factor of 21/2
options.explanation: Increasing the length by a factor of sqrt(2) multiplies the period by the fourth root of 2, not 2.

options.text: Must be increased by a factor 4
options.explanation: Increasing the length by a factor of 4 doubles the period, as the period is proportional to the square root of the length.

questions.explanation: The period of a simple pendulum is given by the formula T = 2π√(L/g), where T is the period, L is the length of the pendulum, and g is the acceleration due to gravity. To double the period (T' = 2T), we need T' = 2π√(L'/g) = 2T = 2(2π√(L/g)). Solving for L', we find L' = 4L, meaning the length must be increased by a factor of 4. Option A is incorrect because increasing the length by a factor of 2 only increases the period by a factor of √2. Option B is incorrect because it would decrease the period. Option C is incorrect because increasing the length by a factor of √2 results in a period increase by the fourth root of 2, not 2.

questions.text: In the time required for the tuning fork to make one complete vibration, the wave in air will travel a distance equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests the wave travels only a quarter of its wavelength in one cycle, which is incorrect because a full cycle corresponds to a full wavelength. The distance traveled by the wave in one period should match the wavelength.

This option suggests the wave travels half of its wavelength in one cycle. While a wave can be at half its wavelength during a cycle, the full distance traveled in one complete cycle is the full wavelength.
This is the correct option. The wave travels a distance equal to one full wavelength in the time it takes for the tuning fork to complete one vibration.
This option suggests the wave travels two full wavelengths in one cycle, which is incorrect. The wave travels only a single wavelength during one complete vibration of the tuning fork.

questions.explanation: In the time required for a tuning fork to make one complete vibration, the wave in air travels a distance equal to one wavelength. This is because the frequency of the tuning fork determines the number of vibrations per second, and the speed of sound in air is constant for a given temperature and pressure. Since speed = frequency × wavelength, the distance covered in one full vibration (or period) is exactly one wavelength. The incorrect options suggest different fractions or multiples of the wavelength, which do not correspond to the physical relationship of wave speed, frequency, and wavelength.

questions.text: Which one of the following contains a pair of transverse and longitudinal wave?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Both radio waves and X-rays are types of electromagnetic waves, which are transverse in nature.
Infrared and ultraviolet waves are both electromagnetic and thus transverse waves.
Sound waves are longitudinal waves, while radio waves are transverse electromagnetic waves.

options.text: Wave in a ripple tank & light
options.explanation: Waves in a ripple tank are surface waves, which have both transverse and longitudinal components, but light is a transverse electromagnetic wave.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: Sound & Radio Waves. Sound waves are longitudinal waves, meaning their oscillations occur parallel to the direction of wave travel. In contrast, radio waves are a type of electromagnetic wave, which are transverse, meaning their oscillations occur perpendicular to the direction of wave travel.Option A and Option B are incorrect because radio, X-rays, infrared, and ultraviolet waves are all electromagnetic and thus transverse.Option D is incorrect because, while waves in a ripple tank can exhibit both transverse and longitudinal characteristics, light is purely a transverse electromagnetic wave.

questions.text: The velocity of a particle moving a frequency 'f' and wave length λ is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct because the velocity v of a wave is given by the product of its frequency (f) and its wavelength (λ).
This is incorrect because dividing frequency by wavelength does not represent velocity. Velocity is the product of frequency and wavelength, not the quotient.
This is incorrect because dividing wavelength by frequency does not represent velocity. It would imply a time-related quantity, not velocity.
This is incorrect because squaring the wavelength and multiplying by frequency does not result in velocity. The correct relationship is a simple product of wavelength and frequency.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is f λ. The velocity (v) of a wave is calculated using the formula v = f × λ, where f is the frequency and λ is the wavelength. This fundamental wave equation relates how fast a wave propagates through a medium. Option A correctly represents this relationship. Option B suggests dividing frequency by wavelength, which is incorrect as it does not conform to the formula for velocity. Option C reverses the roles of wavelength and frequency, which does not apply here. Option D is incorrect because it unnecessarily squares the wavelength, which does not fit the standard wave velocity formula.

questions.text: The one which has the longest wave length in the following is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Red light has a longer wavelength compared to other colors in the visible spectrum but is not the longest overall.
X-rays have very short wavelengths, much shorter than visible light and radio waves.
Infra-red radiation has a longer wavelength than visible light but is shorter than radio waves.
Radio waves have the longest wavelength in the electromagnetic spectrum.

questions.explanation: Radio waves have the longest wavelengths among the options and the entire electromagnetic spectrum. They range from a few millimeters to hundreds of kilometers. Red light and infra-red have longer wavelengths than X-rays but are shorter than radio waves. X-rays, on the other hand, have very short wavelengths, which are shorter than visible light. Thus, the correct answer is radio waves.

questions.text: Which of the following has the shortest wavelength?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Gamma rays have the shortest wavelength in the electromagnetic spectrum, typically less than 10 picometers.
Ultraviolet light has a shorter wavelength than visible light but is longer than gamma rays and X-rays.
Microwaves have longer wavelengths than gamma rays and ultraviolet light, typically ranging from 1 millimeter to 1 meter.
Radio waves have the longest wavelength in the electromagnetic spectrum, ranging from 1 millimeter to 100 kilometers.

questions.explanation: Gamma rays have the shortest wavelength in the electromagnetic spectrum, allowing them to penetrate materials more deeply than other forms of electromagnetic radiation. Ultraviolet light, while shorter in wavelength than visible light, is longer than both gamma rays and X-rays. Microwaves and radio waves have significantly longer wavelengths, with radio waves having the longest wavelength of all.
